title: Returns Rate and Reverse Logistics Cost
spl:
index=business sourcetype="returns_log" earliest=-30d
| stats count as returns, sum(refund_amount) as refund_total by sku, reason_code
| join type=left sku [
    search index=business sourcetype="dbx:shipments" earliest=-30d
    | stats sum(shipped_qty) as shipped_units by sku
]
| eval return_rate_pct=if(shipped_units>0, round(100*returns/shipped_units,2), null())
| sort - refund_total
| table sku, reason_code, shipped_units, returns, return_rate_pct, refund_total
description: Tracks return counts and refund value against shipped units so merchandising sees which products drive margin leakage. We help you trigger quality reviews or sizing guides before return rates damage the brand.
value: Tracks return counts and refund value against shipped units so merchandising sees which products drive margin leakage. We help you trigger quality reviews or sizing guides before return rates damage the brand.
implementation: (1) Load returns authorisations and shipment facts from order management with a shared stock keeping unit key; (2) normalise reason codes to a small taxonomy for reporting; (3) schedule weekly and invite category managers when any stock keeping unit exceeds the agreed return rate.
visualization: Bar chart (return rate by SKU), Table (reason code breakdown), Single value (portfolio return %), Pie chart (reason mix).
knownFalsePositives: Return spikes after product quality issues, sizing or fit problems, or generous holiday return policies — segment by reason codes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=business` `sourcetype="returns_log"` (sku, return_date, refund_amount, reason_code), `index=business` `sourcetype="dbx:shipments"` (sku, shipped_qty, ship_date).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Load returns authorisations and shipment facts from order management with a shared stock keeping unit key; (2) normalise reason codes to a small taxonomy for reporting; (3) schedule weekly and invite category managers when any stock keeping unit exceeds the agreed return rate.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=business sourcetype="returns_log" earliest=-30d
| stats count as returns, sum(refund_amount) as refund_total by sku, reason_code
| join type=left sku [
    search index=business sourcetype="dbx:shipments" earliest=-30d
    | stats sum(shipped_qty) as shipped_units by sku
]
| eval return_rate_pct=if(shipped_units>0, round(100*returns/shipped_units,2), null())
| sort - refund_total
| table sku, reason_code, shipped_units, returns, return_rate_pct, refund_total
```

Understanding this SPL

**Returns Rate and Reverse Logistics Cost** — Tracks return counts and refund value against shipped units so merchandising sees which products drive margin leakage. We help you trigger quality reviews or sizing guides before return rates damage the brand.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=business` `sourcetype="returns_log"` (sku, return_date, refund_amount, reason_code), `index=business` `sourcetype="dbx:shipments"` (sku, shipped_qty, ship_date). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: business; **sourcetype**: returns_log. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=business, sourcetype="returns_log", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by sku, reason_code** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for comparisons across those breakdowns for this use case).
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **return_rate_pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Pipeline stage (see **Returns Rate and Reverse Logistics Cost**): table sku, reason_code, shipped_units, returns, return_rate_pct, refund_total


Step 3 — Validate
Compare return rates and reverse-logistics cost to the ERP, e-commerce, and returns vendor data for the same product families.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (return rate by SKU), Table (reason code breakdown), Single value (portfolio return %), Pie chart (reason mix).
grandmaExplanation: We watch returns and reverse logistics cost so we fix products and processes that create expensive churn.
